basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Māʿūn — “Small Kindnesses” — is a brief sūra that overturns the usual order of things: to deny religion (dīn), it says, is not first a false opinion — it is a conduct. The one who shoves away the orphan, who does not feed the poor, who prays to be seen, who refuses to lend what is useful — that one, in Quranic speech, treats religion as a lie. Tustarī cites a rare saying he attributes to the Psalms of David: “Ask those who stand at the temple, keeping the postures of the worshippers, while their hearts are in the world, whether they take Me lightly, or are trying to deceive Me.” And the Prophet said to Muʿādh when he asked for counsel: “Be sincere to God, and a small amount of deeds will suffice you.” The sūra, by contrast with the Kawthar that precedes it, names the posterity turned back upon itself: one can have all the gestures of devotion and be inwardly cut off. True piety verifies itself in the orphans one looks at, the poor one feeds, the objects one lends.

versets.trad: Have you seen the one who treats religion as a lie?
versets.commentaire: yukadhdhibu bi-d-dīn (يُكَذِّبُ بِالدِّين) — to give the lie to religion, or to the Judgment (dīn means both). Qushayrī notes the form: the verse came down as a rebuke, in astonishment at what the disbeliever does to the orphan. Tustarī suggests, on the first verse, that to deny the Judgment is the root of every other sin and transgression. When the soul ceases to believe there is an Eye on its acts, its acts grow slack. What remains, like an empty shell, is the outward form of piety — described in the verses that follow.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: intensive form of kadhaba (to lie); “to hold as a liar”, “to give the lie to”.
dīn: religion, but also judgment, the debt to God.

versets.trad: That is the one who drives away the orphan,
versets.commentaire: yaduʿʿu (يَدُعُّ) — a harsh verb: to thrust away, to brush off in a single stroke. Qushayrī adds: “God has taken mercy away from his heart; and mercy is taken away only from the heart of a wretched man (shaqī, شَقِيّ).” The presence of the orphan is in reality a sign: the man who cannot bear the orphan's face is the man whose heart has been dried out. Harshness to another is the mark of a more secret hardening. The sūra thus says: show me what a man does to the orphan, and I will tell you where he stands.

mots.def: daʿʿa: to push roughly, to repulse with a stroke.
yatīm: orphan (of the father); figure of absolute vulnerability.

versets.trad: and does not urge the feeding of the indigent.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “He does not urge the feeding of the poor, because he is in the avarice of his soul and in the command of his stinginess.” The verse does not only reproach not giving — it reproaches not urging to give. When the soul is sound, it worries also for the poor that others do not see, it urges others to feed them. When it is sick, it stays sealed in its own self-sufficiency.

mots.def: ḥaḍḍa: to urge, to incite, to exhort to do something.
miskīn: the indigent, the destitute; from the root s-k-n (immobility) — one who no longer has the strength to move.

versets.trad: So woe to those who pray,
versets.commentaire: The verse is famous for its boldness: wayl (وَيْل), “woe”, addressed to those who pray. But Qushayrī stops on a decisive nuance of the next verse: the sūra says ʿan ṣalātihim, “about their prayer” — not fī ṣalātihim, “in their prayer”. The difference is immense: to be heedless in prayer (the mind drifting for an instant) every worshipper knows; were that condemned, none would be safe. But to be heedless about prayer — to delay it, to forget it, to take it lightly — that is what is aimed at.

mots.def: wayl: interjection of threat; among the strongest Quranic expressions.
muṣallī: one who prays; active participle of ṣallā.
mots.gram: preposition + plural active participle, definite genitive

versets.commentaire: Sāhūn ʿan (سَاهُون عَنْ): heedless about the prayer. Tustarī cites a word he takes from the Psalms of David: “Ask those who stand at the temple, keeping the postures of the worshippers, while their hearts are in the world, whether they take Me lightly, or are trying to deceive Me.” And a ḥadīth: “A man does not obtain from his prayer save what he has grasped (mā ʿaqala).” The sūra does not ask for ecstasy; it asks that prayer not be treated as a chore to clear off. It asks that we take it — that the mind be in it for something.

mots.def: masc. plur. relative pronoun: those who.
preposition: about, regarding — not “in”, which would be fī.
sāhī: heedless, forgetful; active participle of sahā.

versets.commentaire: yurāʾūn (يُرَاءُون): they act to be seen. This is riyāʾ (رِيَاء), ostentation — a central fault in Muslim spirituality. Tustarī calls it shirk khafī, “hidden association”: “there are two kinds of association, he writes — that which concerns God Himself, which is not forgiven, and that which concerns one's deeds — as when a man prays or makes the pilgrimage knowing people will speak of him. That too is association.” The ḥadīth that follows: “Be sincere to God, and a small amount of deeds will suffice you.” One act wholly for God is worth more than a thousand cut in half.

mots.def: rāʾā: a derived form of raʾā (to see); “to make oneself seen”, “to act to be seen” — ostentation, riyāʾ.

versets.commentaire: al-māʿūn (المَاعُون). Qushayrī defines it with precision: “like water, fire, pasture, the axe, the cooking-pot, and the other implements of the household; this includes also stinginess and miserliness with anything that can serve creation, within the bounds of what is possible.” A very concrete reading: religion verifies itself in what one lends, what one shares, in the trouble one accepts to take for another in the daily detail. The sūra closes there, on this tiny detail. To refuse the māʿūn is, undramatically, to sign one's inward kufr. And to share it, simply, is to carry — softly, without anyone noticing — true religion.

mots.def: manaʿa: to refuse, to withhold, to keep from access.
māʿūn: what is of use — water, fire, pasture, axe, pot, and every household implement; figuratively, anything that can serve creation and is refused out of avarice.
